title-group.article-title: Hierarchical diversification of instinctual behavior neurons by lineage, birth order, and sex

article-meta.aff: 1.Cellular and Molecular Biology Program, University of Michigan Medical School, Ann Arbor, Michigan 48109

abstract.p: Brain regions devoted to instinctual behaviors, including the vertebrate hypothalamus and arthropod cerebrum, contain bespoke neural circuits dedicated to perceptual and internal regulation of many behavioral states. These circuits are usually complex in structure and contain an extensive diversity of cell types. The regulatory mechanisms that pattern circuits for instinctual behaviors have been challenging to elucidate. Here, we developed methods in Drosophila to transcriptionally profile identified neuronal stem cell lineages in the cerebrum. We applied this method to lineages that generate sex-differentiated neurons with known circuit roles. We identified 91 transcription factors that, in combinations of 6–8, delineate cerebral hemilineages – classes of postmitotic neurons born from the same stem cell and sharing Notch status. Hemilineages comprise the major anatomic classes in the cerebrum and these transcription factors are required to generate their gross features. We further identified 33 transcription factors characteristic of neuronal birth order within lineages; these subtly differentiate neuronal subtypes to provide common computational modules to circuits regulating different behaviors. Our findings suggest that hemilineage and birth order transcription factors operate in a hierarchical system to build, diversify, and sexually differentiate lineally-related neurons that compose complex instinctual circuits.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Higher processing centers in chordate and arthropod brains combine general-purpose learning regions with myriad bespoke circuits that regulate innate behaviors. These two kinds of circuits have distinct logical relationships with neurogenic cell fate diversification programs: In learning circuits, as well as spatially ordered sensory structures like the visual system, a small set of neuron types are specified in very large numbers and wire to generate repetitive columnar or layered structures. In contrast, the neurons and circuits regulating innate behaviors are each built to purpose for a unique information-processing function. Studies of neuronal cell fate diversification have historically focused on repetitive circuits, as these provided the most feasible way to explore general specification principles. The extraordinary diversity of cell types contributing to circuits for motivated behaviors – controlling drives like mating, feeding, aggression, care of offspring, and sleep – has been too difficult to address.

Yet, it is in innate behavior circuits – by definition – where developmental cell fate specification and wiring most directly dictate behavioral function. The genome somehow contains the information to build instinctual meanings of sensory stimuli and relationships among drives into the structure of these neural circuits. To explicate anatomic and functional principles underpinning these circuits and states, there is urgent need to describe the logical principles and molecular mechanisms through which their constituent neurons are patterned during development.

Three axes of diversification produce cell type diversity in the nervous system; they are used distinctly across brain regions within organisms and in different kinds of neurogenesis programs across clades (Holguera and Desplan 2018; Sen 2023). Here, we will determine how these axes pattern instinctual behavior neurons that populate the cerebrum of adult Drosophila melanogaster. First, neural stem cells located in specific positions in space are differentiated from one another (Lichtneckert and Reichert 2008; Urbach and Technau 2008; Briscoe et al. 2000; Jessell 2000; Sagner and Briscoe 2019). In Drosophila, most of the cerebrum derives from ~100 bilaterally symmetric progenitors known as Type I neuroblasts, each giving rise to distinct populations of daughter cells due to this spatial axis (Urbach, Schnabel, and Technau 2003; Urbach and Technau 2004). Second, as a neural stem cell divides asymmetrically, it progresses through expression of different transcription factors (TFs) and RNA binding proteins, and thus produces varied daughter cells over time (Z. Liu et al. 2015; Doe 2017; West et al. 2022; Clark et al. 2019; Lyu et al. 2021a; Roome et al. 2025; Konstantinides et al. 2022; Miyares and Lee 2019; Lyu et al. 2021b). In Drosophila, cerebral Type I neuroblasts divide ~70–100 times spanning neurogenesis windows in the embryo and larva (James W. Truman and Bate 1988). Third, sister neurons born in the same time window can assume distinct fates due to a Notch binary fate switch (Spana and Doe 1996; James W. Truman et al. 2010; Lin et al. 2012; Fiddes et al. 2018; Bello-Rojas and Bagnall 2022; Mark et al. 2021). In Type I lineages, each neuroblast division produces a ganglion mother cell, and these divide once to produce two neurons, who assume Notch-on versus Notch-off fates (Spana and Doe 1996; James W. Truman et al. 2010; Lin et al. 2012; 2010). Sex differentiation provides a fourth axis of neuronal diversification in a subset of cell types.

In Type I lineages of the arthropod central brain and ventral nerve cord, the set of neurons from the same neuroblast that shared the same Notch status form a “hemilineage” (James W. Truman et al. 2010; Lin et al. 2012; Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020). The ~100 Type I neuroblasts of the cerebrum therefore generate ~200 hemilineages; some hemilineages entirely apoptose, and the FlyWire connectome identified 183 morphological hemilineages in the adult central brain (H.-H. Yu et al. 2013; M. Ito et al. 2013; Schlegel et al. 2024; Dorkenwald et al. 2024). There is little active migration in the arthropod brain after neurogenesis, so many neural clones remain close to the sites of their birth. Neurons of the same hemilineage share the same neurite outgrowth tracts and, often, neurotransmitter system, but the two hemilineages from the same stem cell do not appear to share morphological or physiological features (Lacin et al. 2019; Larsen et al. 2009; Spindler and Hartenstein 2010; Lovick et al. 2013; Wong et al. 2013). The somata of paired hemilineages usually neighbor each other, rather than mixing, as neurite outgrowth exerts forces on the somata that pull them apart from one another. Within each hemilineage, sisters born at distinct times appear as anatomic “flavors on a theme” (H.-H. Yu et al. 2010; Lin et al. 2012; Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020). The transcriptional programs producing these major building blocks of the central brain are unknown.

To forge a path into the tremendous lineage diversity of the cerebrum, we rely on some of its best-studied cells and circuits: sex-differentiated populations used for mating (Yamamoto and Koganezawa 2013; Lenschow and Lima 2020; Oren-Suissa and Shirangi 2025; Yapici, Zimmer, and Domingos 2014). Populations of neurons become differentiated through the expression of TFs acting as the terminal effectors of somatic sex differentiation programs (Roggenbuck et al. 2024; Goodwin and Hobert 2021). In Diptera, Doublesex dominates this process in somatic tissues, and Fruitless in the central brain. At least 60 classes of cerebral neurons (~2% of neurons overall) transcribe fruitless, and these classes arise as subtypes of distinct hemilineages (Ryner et al. 1996, 199; H. Ito et al. 1996; Demir and Dickson 2005; Stockinger et al. 2005; Manoli et al. 2005; J. Y. Yu et al. 2010; Cachero et al. 2010; H.-H. Yu et al. 2013). fruitless transcripts are subject to sex-specific splicing to generate male-specific Fruitless (FruM) TFs (Ryner et al. 1996; Demir and Dickson 2005; Goodwin and Hobert 2021; Roggenbuck et al. 2024). Male-typical fruitless splicing is necessary and sufficient to allow male-typical mating behaviors, while the absence of FruM protein is required for female-typical behaviors such as egg laying (Demir and Dickson 2005; Hall 1978; F. Wang et al. 2020; Gill 1963). The sex-specific circuit functions of many fruitless-transcribing neuronal types are known, and constitute a sparse and particularly well-explored subcircuit within the larger cerebrum (K. Kimura et al. 2008; Ruta et al. 2010; Kohl et al. 2013; Clowney et al. 2015; F. Wang et al. 2020; K. Wang et al. 2021; Hoopfer et al. 2015; Manoli and Baker 2004; Lillvis et al. 2024; Koganezawa, Kimura, and Yamamoto 2016).

To ask how the transcriptional identities of fruitless neurons arise from, and relate to, other neurons of their hemilineages, we used a lineage tracing technique to label and isolate neurons derived from reproducibly labeled neuroblasts (Awasaki et al. 2014). Synthesizing transcriptomic, histologic, and genetic approaches, we developed methods to identify and group neurons of the same hemilineage in transcriptomic space. This allowed us to assign diverse transcription factors to their logical roles in postmitotic neurons, i.e. hemilineage patterning, birth order patterning, sex differentiation, and maturation. Overall, we found a group of 91 TFs, highly enriched for homeodomain TFs, that were expressed in combinations of 6 or more across different hemilineages. The TF combinations that exist in the central brain are very sparse compared to those possible, illustrating Waddington’s idea of discontinuous cell types (Waddington 1957). In contrast, 33 TFs, likely reflecting diversification of neurons by birth order, were each expressed in subsets of neurons of each hemilineage and were from the BTB and pipsqueak families, which often have repressor functions (Brovkina et al. 2021; Mance et al. 2024; Park et al. 2024; Bonchuk et al. 2011; Janssens et al. 2022a). Most TFs in each set endured within postmitotic neurons into adulthood and thus barcode neuronal identity.

Using mAL neurons from the Notch-off hemilineage of the CREa1 neuroblast, we probed the requirement for hemilineage and birth order factors in generating gross morphological and functional properties, subtype flavors, and sex-sensitivity. mAL neurons are a fruitless-expressing, GABAergic population that inhibits male courtship initiation in response to gustatory pheromones of males and heterospecific females (K.-I. Kimura et al. 2005; Koganezawa et al. 2010; Clowney et al. 2015; Kallman, Kim, and Scott 2015; Hiroki Ito et al. 2016; Zhang et al. 2018). We identified a set of seven TFs from diverse protein families that mark the hemilineage in general; this TF code likely results from a transcriptional operation that combines neuroblast transcriptional status plus Notch status at neuronal birth. Each factor that we mutated disrupted CREa1B morphology. We found that distinct subsets of fruitless and non-fruitless cells in this hemilineage express distinct birth order TFs, and we could shift subtype fates by manipulating these TFs.

These findings lead us to a model of the relationship between specification of cerebral neuron types and their wiring into instinctual behavior circuits: The gross and unique features of hemilineages are determined by their combination of postmitotic hemilineage TFs, while a transcriptionally orthogonal set of birth-order-specific TFs pattern variation on the hemilineage anatomic theme. The potential for sex differentiation, via transcription of fruitless, derives from these two axes acting in concert and makes the most modest contribution to overall transcriptional state. These axes are arranged hierarchically, with birth order nested inside hemilineage, and sex differentiation acting on specific temporal cohorts within the hemilineage.

sec.title: Transcriptional analysis of developing cerebral lineages
sec.p: To characterize the patterning of instinctual behavior neurons, we sought to durably label model lineages that produce neurons in the well-studied mating circuit (Figure 1A). We used an intersectional lineage tracing approach in which expression of a GAL4 within specific neuroblasts initiates a recombination cascade, resulting in permanent expression of the LexA transcription factor within its progeny (Figure 1B) (Awasaki et al. 2014). We drove lineage tracing using the R19C05 enhancer, a fragment of the Pdfr locus, which has been empirically shown to be expressed in the CREa1 and SMPad1 neuroblasts, among additional lineages (Figure 1B, D) (Q. Ren et al. 2016). CREa1 generates the fruitless mAL type; and SMPad1, the aSP-2 type (J. Y. Yu et al. 2010; Cachero et al. 2010; H.-H. Yu et al. 2013). We found that ~10 lineages were labeled reproducibly, while additional neuroblast clones were labeled sporadically. This method labels all neurons born after the completion of the recombination cascade, thus embryonically-born sisters may not be included.

We FAC-sorted labeled neurons and performed scRNA-seq on early (12hr After Puparium Formation) and mid-pupal (48hrAPF) stages to transcriptionally characterize cerebral lineages during important events in neuronal specification and circuit wiring (Figure 1D). Lineage-specific programmed apoptosis is common in the nervous system, and is often sexually differentiated, including in both mAL and aSP-2 neurons (K.-I. Kimura et al. 2005; von Philipsborn et al. 2014). In a subset of libraries, we therefore genetically blocked the completion, but not the transcriptional induction, of apoptosis by expressing the caspase inhibitor P35 (lexAop-p35) using our lineage labeling strategy (Figure 1C) (Hay, Wolff, and Rubin 1994; Q. Ren et al. 2016). Inclusion of neurons fated to apoptose, which were easily identified by their enduring high-level transcription of pro-apoptotic genes grim, reaper and sickle, provides access to cryptic cell types and fates. At 48h APF, we collected two libraries with the apoptotic block and two without; at 12h APF, we collected two libraries, both with apoptosis blocked (Figure 1D). We removed optic lobe neurons, Kenyon cells, glia, and progenitors from our dataset to allow more nuanced comparisons of cerebral, Type I lineages (see Methods, and Figure S1C). We sequenced male and female neurons together and categorized them post-hoc using expression of the male-specific lncRNAs rox1 and rox2 (Figure S1G–H).

Within this dataset, we include five axes of neuronal diversity: Neuroblast origin, Notch diversification, birth order, sex, and maturation. Clustering using default workflows (highly variable genes across clusters) in Seurat (Hao et al. 2024a) was dominated by processes the cells were experiencing (e.g. apoptotic programs, neurotransmitter expression), rather than by the lineage relationships we sought to understand. We reasoned that measuring variable transcription factor expression would be the best way to describe neuronal diversification events (Flames et al. 2007; Hobert 2016; Sweeney et al. 2018; Yao et al. 2023). Therefore, we used transcripts of the 628 annotated Drosophila transcription factors, including fruitless, to perform semi-supervised clustering of the full dataset. After integrating time points, we tested a variety of methods and resolutions to probe how these axes of diversity related to patterns of transcription factor expression in the dataset. Surprisingly, we found that neurons did not separate by sex except at the highest resolutions; this suggests that our semi-supervised analysis method had identified durable transcription factor fingerprints associated with lineage, Notch status, and birth order. We selected an intermediate resolution based on standard bioinformatic tools, producing 52 clusters. These include some subdivisions of the 20 reproducibly labeled hemilineages, as well as clusters with lower stoichiometry likely to represent sporadically labeled lineages (see Methods and Figure 1E, S1F).

We next identified cell types that we knew were present in our dataset using expression of known markers (Figure 1G): The Notch-ON CREa1 hemilineage (CREa1A) comprises DAT-expressing dopaminergic neurons, while the Notch-OFF hemilineage (CREa1B) contains the fruitless-expressing, GABAergic mAL neurons (Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020). Cluster 14 was GABAergic, contained a subset of fru-expressing neurons, and included female cells that had activated the pro-apoptotic transcriptional program; we thus hypothesized that this cluster represented the CREa1B hemilineage, and that the subset of fruitless neurons within it were mAL neurons, most of which typically apoptose in females (K.-I. Kimura et al. 2005). We found that DAT expression was confined to cluster 1, which must therefore contain the CREa1A hemilineage. Cluster 1 also contained a set of apoptosing cells that derived only from the libraries containing p35 and that expressed GABAergic enzymes; we manually split this cluster into CREa1A versus the apoptosing hemilineage (Figure S1I and see Methods). We also manually split a cluster containing two well-studied antennal lobe lineages that were merged in our UMAP (Figure S1J and see Methods). We next tracked the progeny of SMPad1: The Notch-OFF SMPad1 hemilineage (SMPad1B) undergoes apoptosis, while the Notch-ON hemilineage (SMPad1A) is glutamatergic and contains fruitless-expressing aSP-2 neurons, which we find apoptose in females. Cluster 22 matched the expected characteristics of SMPad1A. While we didn’t use neurotransmitter transcripts in clustering, we found that most clusters in our dataset expressed unified neurotransmitter systems, consistent with correspondence to hemilineages (Lacin et al. 2019). We therefore adopted the working hypothesis that hemilineage [lineage x Notch status] produces the primary variation in transcription factor expression across developing central brain neurons. Intriguingly, this is consistent with the idea that hemilineages provide the major anatomic building blocks of the arthropod brain and ventral nerve cord (Harris et al. 2015).

sec.title: A unique transcription factor combination defines a model hemilineage
sec.p: Due to the extensive prior knowledge of the circuit function and sex differentiation of mAL neurons, we used CREa1B as a model to interrogate patterning of instinctual behavior neurons (K.-I. Kimura et al. 2005; Clowney et al. 2015; Kallman, Kim, and Scott 2015; Koganezawa et al. 2010; Koganezawa, Kimura, and Yamamoto 2016, Zhang et al., 2018). To test the hypothesis that our transcriptional clusters represent anatomic hemilineages, we first identified TFs enriched in CREa1B: Fer1 (bHLH family, homologue of vertebrate PTF1A), TfAP-2 (AP-2 family, AP-2 homologue), fd59A (forkhead, FoxD3 homologue), dsf (nuclear receptor, NR2E1/TLX homologue), tup (LIM homeobox, ISL1 homologue) and ara and caup (both TALE homeobox, homologous to IRX4) (Figure 2A,B S2D). Strikingly, these seven TFs were expressed consistently across developmental time, and none of them labeled the CREa1A hemilineage (Figure 2A, B). CREa1A was marked by Scro, dmrt99B, erm, D, Lim1, hth, SoxN, Fer2 and CG9932 (Figure 2J, S2I). The zinc finger TFs salm and salr were also enriched in CREa1B, but only labeled a subset of cells (Figure S2E); these likely reflect more complex regulation along both hemilineage and birth order axes. The presence of dsf in CREa1B and Fer2 in CREa1A are consistent with prior studies (Bou Dib et al. 2014; Duckhorn et al. 2022). Notably, dsf mutant males court promiscuously, consistent with a requirement for Dsf in mAL neuron function (Finley et al. 1997).

We used a set of knock-in alleles in TfAP-2, dsf, and fd59a, and generated a T2A-GAL4 knockin translational reporter of Fer1, to examine which neurons express each of these factors over space and time (Figure 2E, S2A–C, S2G). Across the brain, each marked a different set of other major tracts and clumps of neuronal somata, consistent with expression in diverse hemilineages and with previous studies that have identified “packets” of cerebral neurons sharing the same transcription factor (Pereanu and Hartenstein 2006). We used these morphologies, combined with RNA FISH, to validate that this transcriptional cluster corresponds to the morphological CREa1B cluster within the brain (Figure 2C–H, Figure S2A–C). All four TFs were expressed universally in the postembryonic CREa1B clone. Moreover, all four were expressed from 12h APF to adulthood, consistent with recent descriptions of hemilineage-specific transcription factors in the VNC (Figure 2I, S2D) (Soffers et al. 2025). Cross-checking expression patterns in an adult dataset of fruitless neurons, mAL neurons from CREa1B and PAM DANs from CREa1A continued to robustly express all the transcription factors which define them at 12 and 48h APF (MV Brovkina and EJ Clowney, manuscript in preparation). Pairs among these TFs typically labeled additional hemilineages (Figure 2I). Hemilineages sharing three factors with CREa1B were rare, and we never identified a hemilineage that shared 4 or more with CREa1B. These results support that the combination of our labeling strategy and our bioinformatic approach drives cells into their hemilineages and allows us to test the hypothesis that combinations of TFs expressed in postmitotic neurons produce hemilineage characteristics.

Are these hemilineage TFs inherited from the CREa1 neuroblast? We used lineage tracing to ask whether any deadpan-positive neuroblasts had ever expressed these TFs (Awasaki et al. 2014). We found that TfAP-2 induced labeling of other neuroblast clones, but not CREa1 (Figure S2F). Fer1 did not induce labeling of neuroblast clones, suggesting it is not expressed in Dpn+ progenitors. Lineage tracing neuroblasts positive for dsf produced pervasive neuronal labeling (Figure S2F), resembling the pattern we saw when lineage tracing all neuroblasts, using tubulin-GAL4 (Figure S2F). Therefore, dsf is likely expressed in most or all neuroblasts at an early developmental time point, but isn’t selective for CREa1. On the other hand, CREa1 and many other neuroblasts whose progeny are included in our dataset express vnd (Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020), yet we didn’t observe any vnd expression in our postmitotic neurons (Figure S2H). Together, these results suggest that CREa1B is defined by expression of a suite of TFs that are induced in postmitotic cells and continue to barcode these cells into adulthood (Enriquez et al. 2015; Özel et al. 2022; Soffers et al. 2025). This code is not simply inherited from the neuroblast and GMC, as the hemilineage TF combinations expressed postmitotically are distinct from those in the neuroblast and in CREa1A neurons and must therefore be induced downstream of each unique [spatial x Notch] state.

sec.title: Complex transcription factor combinations uniquely define hemilineages
sec.p: Arthropod hemilineage identity links neurogenic origins to adult anatomy and connectivity: Years of neuroanatomical and neurodevelopmental studies have determined which neuroblasts generate adult neuronal hemilineages of the cerebrum, and recent connectomic datasets include hemilineage identity as metadata (H.-H. Yu et al. 2010; Lin et al. 2012; H.-H. Yu et al. 2013; M. Ito et al. 2013; Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020; Schlegel et al. 2024; Dorkenwald et al. 2024). Moreover, individual hemilineage anatomic units are evolutionarily ancient, and can frequently be matched across divergent arthropod species (Thomas et al. 1984; J. W. Truman and Ball 1998). Adding transcriptional fingerprints to these anatomic descriptions would allow hemilineage categorization to serve as a Rosetta Stone for solving the developmental events that build instinctual neurons and circuits of the cerebrum, especially if these fingerprints are durable across the neuron’s lifetime. We therefore sought definitive TF combinations for the rest of the hemilineages in our dataset, which represents ~10% of central brain, Type I hemilineages at full stoichiometry and ~25% if we include sporadically labeled clones.

To do this, we first identified marker genes for our clusters from our integrated dataset using Seurat and then filtered the list for only transcription factors. Of 628 annotated TFs in the Drosophila genome, 103 were markers of subsets of our clusters (Supplemental Table 1); the majority had cluster-defining expression patterns, similar to what we demonstrated for the CREa1B-enriched TFs (e.g. see fkh, Fer2 and otp in Figure 3). Of 102 homeodomain TFs annotated in the genome by FlyBase, 51 were included among the 103 cluster-defining TFs (Bürglin and Affolter 2016). This three-fold enrichment corresponds to the well-documented role of homeodomain TFs in neuronal diversification (Reilly et al. 2020; Briscoe et al. 2000; Xu et al. 2023). As we describe above for CREa1B, the rest of the genes are from diverse TF families. Many of the TFs in this list also overlapped with hemilineage-defining TFs in the VNC (Lacin, Zhu, et al. 2014; Allen et al. 2020; Soffers et al. 2025). Using these TF predictions along with expression patterns from the literature and/or additional histological follow-up, we were able to confirm several other matches between transcriptional clusters and anatomic hemilineages (Figure 3A, C–G).

In parallel, we blinded ourselves to TF identities and then manually inspected expression patterns of all 628 TFs across our cluster map split by developmental stage. We called 124 TFs as selective for subsets of hemilineages, of which 54 had a homeodomain fold. 33 of these 124 TFs had complex expression, i.e. they marked only precise neuronal subsets (e.g. Clk, foxo); marked cell classes we had mostly filtered out of our dataset (dac in Kenyon cells, gcm1 and gcm2 in glia); or reflected aspects of both lineage and birth order (e.g. Dbx, RunxA, svp, luna). We therefore retained 91 simple hemilineage TFs, of which 49 have a homeodomain fold (Figure 3A). Of the rest of the TFs in the genome, >200 were expressed ubiquitously, >150 were negligibly expressed, ~50 were associated with maturation stage, ~30 appeared associated with birth order (as we describe below) and the remainder had expression patterns that seemed a product of more than one differentiation/diversification axis (e.g. NK7.1). Our manual descriptions of the behavior of each TF in the fly genome is provided in Supplemental Table 2 and select scRNAseq expression patterns are shown in Figure S3D.

We next examined how these TF sets behaved using metrics of cell-type-specificity versus maturation-specificity, as in (Jain et al. 2022); our hemilineage TFs were cell-type-specific but varied little across the maturation axis (Figure 3B). We inspected expression of each TF across our dataset of adult fruitless neurons (Brovkina and Clowney, manuscript in preparation). These adult fru neurons derive from >60 hemilineages and we have used a similar transcription-factor-based clustering to analyze them. We found that the TFs that behaved “hemilineage-ly” in our 12h APF and 48h APF neuroblast clones also marked sparse clusters in overlapping combinations in these adult cells, and we could easily match fru cell type across datasets using our TF combination fingerprints, as for mAL neurons, above. Using the TF codes we define here, we were also able to pick out specific hemilineages across disparate published datasets, even when those datasets were underpowered to separate neuronal subtypes (Figure 3SA–B) (Davie et al. 2018) .

While making anatomic-transcriptional matches for all the hemilineages in our dataset is beyond the scope of this manuscript, the procedure we used here can be generally deployed by other researchers with specific interests and knowledge. We hypothesize that the combinations of hemilineage-specific TFs expressed in postmitotic neurons are required to give neurons within hemilineages their shared characteristics and makes them different from other hemilineages.

sec.title: Identification of an orthogonal postmitotic TF code associated with neuronal birth order
sec.p: In our examination of transcription factor expression patterns, we noticed a subset of TFs that were expressed in swathes of neurons within each hemilineage cluster (Figure 4B). A universal birth order code for postmitotic central brain neurons has not been previously described, to our knowledge. To identify additional factors with this expression pattern, we measured variance of each of the 628 TFs across clusters, within clusters, and along the maturation axis (Figure 4A). We identified >30 TFs with high variance within clusters and low variance across clusters, and cross-checked this with our manual annotations above. Through this approach, we identified a total of 33 factors, including br, pdm3, dan, danr, hb, bab1, bab2, Eip93F, ab, mamo, dati, and chinmo. The RNA binding protein Imp was expressed in a similar pattern, while Syp was universally expressed (Z. Liu et al. 2015) (Figure S4D). Several of these genes have been shown to act as temporal factors in neuroblasts and/or to label subsets of post-embryonically-born neurons within the same VNC or central brain lineage (Z. Liu et al. 2015; Lin et al. 2012; Zhu et al. 2006; L.-Y. Liu et al. 2019; Maurange, Cheng, and Gould 2008; Syed, Mark, and Doe 2017; Kohwi, Hiebert, and Doe 2011). This birth order series was distinct from the temporal progression of intermediate neural progenitors (INPs) in Type II lineages and had only minimal overlap with temporal TFs in postmitotic optic lobe neurons (Bayraktar and Doe 2013; Konstantinides et al. 2022; Tang et al. 2022; Simon et al. 2025). Some factors were expressed consistently across 12 and 48h APF stages, while others were enriched at one or the other (Figure S4C). Most continued to be expressed in matched types of adult neurons (Brovkina and Clowney, manuscript in preparation), and therefore cannot simply be leftovers from the neuroblast. We hypothesized that these TFs select for distinct terminal identities within a hemilineage according to the order that neurons are born.

We next wanted to verify that putative birth order factors truly label subsets of neurons across many hemilineages. Br and Pdm3 have previously been shown to allocate postmitotic neurons to alternative fates (Smolin et al. 2024; Özel et al. 2022). We focused on their expression, along with Mamo and Imp, in our R19C05-traced hemilineages. Using immunohistochemistry, Br and Pdm3 labeled subsets of neurons within hemilineages throughout the brain and had nearly mutually exclusive expression (Figure 4E). The 33 putative birth order TFs are enriched for annotated transcriptional repressors and many contain BTB (ab, br, mamo, chinmo) or pipsqueak (dan, danr, Eip93F) domains, or both (psq, CG3726, bab1, bab2) (Figure 4F–G) (Brovkina et al. 2021; Mance et al. 2024; Park et al. 2024; Bonchuk et al. 2011; Janssens et al. 2022a).

sec.title: Hemilineage TFs are independently required for shared hemilineage features
sec.p: We hypothesize that hemilineage TF combinations are important to specify hemilineage development. To address this, we focused on CREa1B. We examined null mutants of fd59a and dsf, which survive to adulthood, and used the 19C05 driver to generate CREa1B clones with TfAP-2 knockdown. In all three cases, we see a clear disruption to CREa1B development: Removing fd59A or TfAP-2 produced severed morphological defects, where the typical axonal tracts and dendrites were often completely gone (Figure 5A&E). In dsf mutants, CREa1B neurite tracts are intact, but dendritic and axonal anatomy are disrupted (Figure 5I). In each case morphological disruptions are shared by fru and non-fru neurons within CREa1B, suggesting these factors regulate morphology similarly across neuronal subtypes within a hemilineage.

We also asked how each factor impacted the number of cells within CREa1B and distribution of fru expression. Our previous work suggested that fru is induced by distinct regulatory mechanisms across different cell types (Brovkina et al. 2021), and here we did not find any one-to-one correspondence of hemilineage or birth order TFs with fru expression (Figure 3 & 4). In dsf mutants, we saw that the overall number of CREa1B cells decreased, while the number of fru cells increased (Figure 5J, Figure S5C). In fd59a mutants, we saw modest and sex-specific changes in the distribution of fru and non-fru cells in the hemilineage (Figure 5B, Fig S5A). In our TfAP-2 knockdown CREa1B clones, we see a severe drop in the number of fru mAL neurons in both sexes, and a corresponding drop in the overall number of neurons in the hemilineage in males (Figure 5F, Figure S5B). Most mAL neurons are programmed to apoptose, but are spared in males by FruM’s suppression of pro-apoptotic gene transcription (Figure 1C) (K.-I. Kimura et al. 2005). This normally results in the retention of ~30 mAL neurons in males, and only ~5 in females (K.-I. Kimura et al. 2005). The most parsimonious explanation of the TfAP-2 knockdown data (loss of fru expression in both sexes, severe loss of neurons only in male) is that TfAP-2 is required in CREa1B for fru expression. In females, loss of fru transcription is observed; in males, loss of fru and FruM unmasks the apoptotic program, reducing the size of the hemilineage.

These results confirm that hemilineage-specific combinations of transcription factors are critical for the development of neuronal features. For TfAP-2, the effects we observe must be cell autonomous in postmitotic neurons, as we knocked the gene down clonally and we know it is not expressed in the CREa1 progenitor. We suspect that the complex Dsf phenotype may be due to both fate specification events in the CREa1 progenitor and regulation of terminal feature development in neurons, and will test this hypothesis elsewhere.

sec.title: Birth order factors differentiate mAL neurons and sex-shared sisters
sec.p: Next, we asked whether putative birth order factors can delineate CREa1B neuronal subtypes. Different hemilineages produce different numbers of neuronal subtypes, up to 40 unique types produced by the antennal lobe lineages (H.-H. Yu et al. 2010; Lin et al. 2012; Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020). 9 morphological subtypes of CREa1B neurons have been identified and ordered using tsMARCM birthdating (Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020). We subclustered CREa1B and found 7 transcriptional clusters across our integrated timepoints (Figure 6B). Given CREa1B is a sexually dimorphic hemilineage, we first broadly split CREa1B subtypes into fru mAL neurons and non-fru sisters. We combed the literature for cells with morphology resembling the non-fru sisters and identified the mlSEZt neurons, which are labeled by R29F12 (NetA fragment)-R55E01 (CG15803 fragment) split Gal4 (Deere et al. 2023). These neurons appear to play an analogous circuit role to mAL neurons, gating feeding in response to flavors, where mAL neurons gate mating in response to pheromone tastes (Deere et al. 2023; Clowney et al. 2015; Kallman, Kim, and Scott 2015; Snell et al. 2022; Chen and Dahanukar 2020). Clusters 5 and 6 expressed NetA and CG15803 but lack fru, suggesting these are the mlSEZt’s (Figure 6B). We then compared the anatomy of mlSEZt neurons to mAL neurons labeled using a fru reporter; we confirmed that these are non-overlapping populations, and saw that the morphology of the non-fru mlSEZt neurons of both sexes is remarkably similar to the morphology of the fru mAL neurons of females, which also lack FruM (Figure 6A). We note that there are several other non-fru CREa1B neuronal subtypes visible in EM connectomes, in tsMARCM data, and in the expression patterns of our driver lines. Based on birth ordering from tsMARCM and other heat-shock-based methods, unidentified sisters with complex dendritic “wings” are born first, then the mlSEZt’s, then the fru mAL’s, and then one other unnamed, non-fru sister type, which has antler-shaped axons and truncated dendrites (Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020). Antler and winged types are likely contained within CREa1B subclusters 1 and 2; our transcriptional analysis is underpowered to subdivide the winged types, which each occur only once per hemisphere.

To ask about the transcriptional diversity of mAL neurons, we looked at fru expression in our CREa1B cluster and found that mAL neurons are split into clusters 3 and 4 (Fig 6B). Each of these clusters contained cells from males and females, suggesting that these clusters are not yet split by events downstream of FruM. We found that mAL and mlSEZt neurons could be subdivided by distinct birth order factors: mlSEZt neurons were enriched for rn and mamo, mAL cluster 4 for br and Eip93F and mAL cluster 3 for pdm3, dan and danr (Figure 6C&F). By immunohistochemistry, we found that most mAL neurons expressed either Pdm3 or Br, and not both, demonstrating that these are bone fide mAL subsets (Figure 4E, 6E). Interestingly, we found that the Drosophila proapoptotic genes grim and skl were enriched in the pdm3 subset versus the br set, and that nearly all the pdm3-expressing female cells arose from the libraries in which we blocked apoptosis (Figure 6F, S6B). Thus we infer that pdm3 mAL neurons are the ones that initiate programmed cell death, which is blocked by FruM in male, while br mAL neurons are those normally retained in females (K.-I. Kimura et al. 2005). As Kimura 2005 showed that the apoptosing fru neurons are the later-born, we conclude that br cells are born first, and then pdm3 cells; though tsMARCM birth dating in females leaves open the possibility that these fates alternate (Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020).

To view these distinct mAL clusters in the brain, we used split-GAL4 drivers to intersect pdm3 or br expression with hemilineage-defining TfAP-2. We found that the TfAP-2, pdm3-expressing mAL set was indeed absent in the female; in the male, all TfAP-2, pdm3 cells had the male-typical “horsetail” shape (Figure 6G). Intersection of br and TfAP-2 included both sex-differentiated and sex-shared CREa1B cells, consistent with our transcriptional data, in which br expression straddled the transition from non-fru to fru. We verified that non-apoptosing female mAL neurons were within the br set by intersecting br, TfAP-2, and fru (Figure 6G). Blocking cell death in CREa1B equalized the proportion of mAL types between the sexes (Figure S6C).

We compared transcription between the pdm3 versus br mAL subsets (Figure 6F). Strikingly, the pdm3 sets from males and from females in which apoptosis was blocked expressed the neuropeptide Dh31, while the br sets did not; thus when the pdm3 cells apoptose in females, Dh31 expression is completely lost (Figure 6F, H S6A). Together, these results suggest that the main role for FruM in the pdm3 mAL subset is to block apoptosis by suppressing expression of grim and skl, and that Dh31 peptide will be male-specific in wild type mAL neurons due to regulation of apoptosis by FruM, not direct induction of Dh31 (Figure S6A&B). On the other hand, seven genes were downregulated in male versus female br mAL cells (Figure 6H, right, Figure S6D). These included a neuronal adhesion molecule (DIP-delta), a neuropeptide (Nplp1) and an RNA-binding protein (mbl). Because we showed previously that FruM represses its direct targets, these male-suppressed genes are consistent with direct regulation by Fruitless protein to sexually differentiate this mAL subtype (Brovkina et al. 2021). Overall, we see that the difference in transcription between birth-order subtypes within a hemilineage (i.e. mlSEZt, versus mAL pdm3, vs mAL br) is greater than differences between sexes (i.e. mAL br male versus female, mAL pdm3 male versus female). This suggests sexual differentiation of neurons is tweaking a sex-shared ground plan rather than producing a completely different cell type in the male.

sec.title: Birth order TFs program hemilineage subtypes
sec.p: To ask whether manipulating the birth order factors within a hemilineage can shift the fates of neurons, we overexpressed pdm3 in CREa1B cells using dsf-intersect-TfAP2 split-GAL4. We verified that this genetic strategy was restricted to postmitotic neurons by using the split-GAL4 to drive lineage labeling using the Awasaki strategy; no lineages were labeled. We found that overexpression of pdm3 altered the total number of fruitless mAL neurons in the female but not the male, suggesting Pdm3 induces the cell death fate and FruM protein is sufficient to block it even in a wider window, i.e. in cells that would usually be of the br mAL type (Figure 7A&A’). Indeed, we saw a reduction in the total number of CREa1B neurons, suggesting Pdm3 could induce cell death outside of the fru window (Figure S7A). However, we did not see a complete loss of cells, indicating that Pdm3 is insufficient to induce cell death in the entire CREa1B hemilineage (Figure S7A). We also see loss of the typical “mlSEZt” ring-shaped axon, loss of “wing” dendrites, and increase in the “antler” CREa1B type upon pdm3 overexpression (Figure 7A&A’). To ask whether the order TFs regulate the expression of each other as they can do in VNC neuroblasts and in the visual system, we examined the effect of pdm3 overexpression on Br expression (Doe 2017; Konstantinides et al. 2022). We found that Br protein persists in CREa1B despite overexpressing pdm3 (Figure S7B&B’), suggesting that these proteins can be co-expressed. However, this does not rule out regulatory relationships among the 33 order TFs in general. Overall, our data suggest that Pdm3 could be important for cell death within CREa1B and for the specification of late-born mAL and antler CREa1B neuronal subtypes at the expense of earlier-born wing and mlSEZt types.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Here we describe a model for the diversification of rare neuronal types with unique behavioral roles. While instinctual behaviors are well studied at the circuit and behavioral level, the transcriptional logic generating these immensely diverse cell types has been obscure. By capturing and sequencing postmitotic neurons of identified Type I lineages of the arthropod cerebrum, we identified two orthogonal TF codes, one of which diversifies hemilineages, and the other the sister neurons within hemilineages. The hemilineage transcription factor combinations that we find in postmitotic neurons are distinct from those patterning the stem cells that generate them, while the birth order code shares many members with other temporal regulatory events in neurogenesis and metamorphosis. Expression of most hemilineage and birth order transcription factors we identify is durable within specific populations of neurons from before metamorphosis through adulthood and thus serve as barcodes of identity both in a causal developmental sense and from our position as observers of the brain. Our model therefore decomposes these “terminal selectors” or “morphology transcription factors” into two groups defined by the separable developmental axes that induce them (Enriquez et al. 2015; Hobert 2016). Finally, both hemilineage and birth order factors set the table that sex differentiation acts on (Roggenbuck et al. 2024).

sec.title: Transcriptional patterning and circuit wiring of lineally related neurons
sec.p: The developmental axes that dominate diversification of transcription, anatomy, and physiology differ across regions of the arthropod brain and generate distinct circuit wiring patterns. Anterior brain lineages occur only once per hemisphere and thus serve singular computational roles, but the dominance of hemilineage in their patterning resembles recent findings in the ventral nerve cord (Urbach, Jussen, and Technau 2016; Allen et al. 2020; Soffers et al. 2025). In contrast, the degree of spatial diversification of neurons in the arthropod visual system is lower, while the transcriptional and morphological differences produced across birth orders is higher (Konstantinides et al. 2022; Özel et al. 2022; Simon et al. 2025).

At the circuit level, work in the VNC has shown that hemilineages can act as functional units and that temporal identity diversifies their connections (Harris et al. 2015; Mark et al. 2021). In line with this model, mlSEZt neurons and the sexually dimorphic mAL neurons within the cerebral CREa1B hemilineage are inhibitory neurons that relay taste information from the subesophageal zone to neurons in the superior lateral protocerebrum. Shared hemilineage identity – encoded by TFs including Dsf, TfAP-2, Fd59a, and Fer1 – provides CREa1B neurons with their major neurite outgrowth tract and physiological computation; their birth order identity – encoded by expression of Rn and Mamo versus Pdm3 or Br – specifies the fine shape of their axons and dendrites and thus their precise connectivity and behavioral role. The result is that male mAL neurons receive gustatory pheromone inputs and project to P1 neurons to gate mating initiation, while mlSEZt neurons of both sexes receive flavor information and connect to non-P1 downstream targets to gate feeding (Clowney et al. 2015; Kallman, Kim, and Scott 2015; Sengupta et al. 2022; Deere et al. 2023). Identifying whether the female mAL neurons are functionally distinct from the morphologically similar, sex-shared mlSEZt’s would shed light on the neural circuitry underlying female behaviors and the role of lineage in circuit wiring. Where these sister neurons of cerebral hemilineages supply a common computational module to distinct circuits, the columnar organization of visual system circuits results from lineally related neurons wiring together. Across species, a variety of developmental relationships are likely exploited to produce circuits with varied connectivity logic.

sec.title: Extant TF codes vs potential TF codes
sec.p: We have not found factors in postmitotic neurons that could separate the spatial identity of their neuroblast of origin versus Notch status. Notch effectors were no longer visible in postmitotic neurons at the time points we analyzed, and hemilineage pairs from the same neuroblast (e.g. CREa1A, B) did not share TFs with each other. In neuroblasts, the expression patterns of dozens of TFs have been shown to correlate with spatial position (i.e. A-P, D-V, medial-lateral) (Urbach and Technau 2004). We have not yet identified TFs participating in hemilineage codes that reflect simple spatial axes. Moreover, we generally did not see the same spatial factors in neuroblasts and in postmitotic hemilineages: vnd is expressed in many of the neuroblasts whose progeny we analyzed, yet was not detected in postmitotic neurons (Y.-J. Lee et al. 2020). TfAP-2 and Fer1 were expressed in postmitotic CREa1B cells, but not in the CReA1 stem cell. While this will require further study, these results together suggest that there is a “translation event” at neurogenesis whereby the spatial factors in the neuroblast combine with the Notch switch to produce unique hemilineage TF codes (Mark et al. 2021; Sen 2023). Kenyon cell neurogenesis, which does not involve Notch diversification and thus does not produce hemilineages, may be an exception that proves the rule–KC neuroblasts and postmitotic KCs share expression of eyeless (Noveen, Daniel, and Hartenstein 2000; Kurusu et al. 2000). On the other hand, type I neuroblasts and differentiating daughters appear to use many of the same temporal factors; whether these are strung together in the same order and whether the temporal code in the daughter matches the temporal code in the neuroblast at the time of its birth, or is offset, remains to be seen.

We were struck by the apparent excess of TFs devoted to hemilineage patterning: The 91 postmitotic hemilineage TFs we identify, taken 7 at a time, could generate nearly a billion unique combinations, yet there are only 200 cerebral hemilineages. Thus, the TF combinations that exist in the brain are a very sparse subset of the combinations that could be formed from the individual factors; this excess diversity explains why we could recover most TFs with hemilineage-specific expression from our initial subsampling of lineages. Recent analyses in the visual system have found that the amount of transcriptional diversity that could be produced by the available transcription factor set is also far in excess of the number of cell types actually made (Simon et al. 2025). Because we observe heterogeneity in the rate at which each is expressed (e.g. from >⅓ of hemilineages for Lim1 or bi to <1/10% for croc and unc-4) and repeated relationships among certain groups of TFs (ara, caup, mirr; CG4328, Lmx1a; Fer1, dsf), combinations that actually exist are unlikely to be a random subsample of those possible. Finally, mutating single hemilineage factors did not cause conversion to a different cell type/hemilineage (Arlotta and Hobert 2015). Instead, we observed generally perturbed axons and dendrites, suggesting that hemilineage factors, while independently required, are sufficient to build ground plans only in specific cocktails. Hemilineages are deeply conserved in insects and can be identified in disparate clades by their characteristic neurite outgrowth tracts (Thomas et al. 1984; J. W. Truman and Ball 1998). By adjusting effector genes downstream of these handles, evolution can subtly alter the terminal characteristics of neuronal development; by altering the handles themselves, evolution could produce novel neuronal ground plans.

sec.title: Development reuses TFs to generate diverse neurons
sec.p: To our knowledge, the temporal transcription factor series patterning larvally born neurons of the cerebrum has not yet been identified. The 33 factors we highlight here are strong candidates based on their expression patterns and because several have already been shown to be important neuroblast factors and/or to have roles in temporal patterning, including chinmo, Eip93F, mamo, br, dan, and hb (Syed, Mark, and Doe 2017; L.-Y. Liu et al. 2019; Kohwi and Doe 2013; Zhu et al. 2006). Some of our birth order transcription factors (br and pdm3) and our hemilineage factors (TfAP-2, fd59A) have been identified as terminal selectors in the optic lobe (Konstantinides et al. 2018; 2022; Özel et al. 2022; Smolin et al. 2024; Simon et al. 2025). These data indicate that, during development, the same transcription factors can occupy different roles across different parts of the nervous system to diversify neuronal types. Moreover, as we describe in Figures 3 and 4, not all transcription factors expressed in subsets of hemilineages or subsets of neurons within them can be assigned to a single diversification axis—various factors reflect more than one axis (e.g. [hemilineage x birth order], [birth order x maturation]). Factors such as Clock and Fru are induced with exquisite specificity, likely downstream of the hemilineage and birth order axes, in neuronal types that form subcircuits relevant to specific classes of behaviors. Finally, different cerebral lineages produce very different degrees of neuronal diversity, up to the ~40 anatomically distinct cell types from antennal lobe lineages (H.-H. Yu et al. 2010; Lin et al. 2012). Such stem cells have been shown to produce more ground plan variety than the “two hemilineages per neuroblast” rule, and may thus rely on additional transcriptional substructure.

sec.title: Lineage-dependent induction and function of Fruitless
sec.p: Through our analysis of sexually differentiated hemilineages, we did not find hemilineage or birth order TFs or combinations universally correlated with fruitless across cell types. We found that fruitless can be expressed in both A and B hemilineages, in all the cells of a hemilineage, in one birth order window, or in multiple. Fruitless being expressed in just a subset of neurons within a hemilineage could indicate that it is repressed in non-fruitless-expressing neurons, possibly due to the action of birth order factors. Overall, our data suggest that there are many ways to achieve fruitless expression, like neurotransmitter identity (Konstantinides et al. 2018; Serrano-Saiz et al. 2020; Kim et al. 2020), and this is in support of our previous findings that diverse enhancers within the fruitless locus drive expression in distinct fruitless cell types (Brovkina et al. 2021). It also supports the idea that fru expression is more akin to a terminal feature of diverse neurons than it is a patterning factor.

Transcriptional sex differences are like a mirage, mostly created by wholesale removal of certain neuron types. Our approach of restoring lost cells by blocking cell death allowed us to identify truly analogous cell types between the sexes. Using this method, we found that Fruitless sexually differentiates neurons differently even across temporal windows of the same hemilineage. The transcriptional differences between matched cell types that are actually present in both sexes are remarkably subtle. Indeed, during our analysis we found greater transcriptional differences across hemilineage cell types (birth order windows) versus for identical [hemilineage x birth order] types across sexes. In male versus female fruitless neurons of the same hemilineage and birth order, we found on the order of 10 genes to be differentially expressed; these were related to cell death (grim and rpr), synapse organization (DIP-delta), and intriguingly, RNA regulation (mbl).

sec.title: Transcriptional mechanisms of cerebral neuron diversification
sec.p: How do hemilineage and birth order TFs generate the hierarchical diversity of neurons regulating instinctual behaviors? In the cerebrum, we found that manipulation of shared hemilineage factors had dramatic effects on neuronal morphology, with a complete loss of both axons and dendrites in some cases, while manipulation of a birth order transcription factor altered the “flavor” of the neuron while preserving the overall theme of the hemilineage. Given these phenotypes, we favor the hypothesis that hemilineage TF cocktails function in concert to activate enhancers needed to pattern gross neuronal ground plans and functional features. In contrast, we found BTB/POZ and pipsqueak transcription factors to be enriched within the order transcription factors. Methodologically diverse recent studies, including our own, have shown that Fruitless and other BTB/POZ TFs in the Tramtrack family are strong repressors (Brovkina et al. 2021; Janssens et al. 2022b; Mance et al. 2024; Park et al. 2024; Bonchuk et al. 2011). We posit that sex differentiation and birth order differentiation, while induced by distinct upstream mechanisms, use the same molecular mechanism – closing hemilineage-specific enhancers – and produce analogous developmental impacts – patterning neuronal variation on the hemilineage theme (Sen et al. 2019). In other parts of the nervous system, where sister neurons are far more different from each other, a different regulatory logic may apply. Alternatively, researchers working in the neural tube proposed the model that enhancer opening is performed in common across lineages, and that spatial diversification of neurons occurs entirely through transcriptional repression (Bailey et al. 2006; Muhr et al. 2001). To discriminate between these models will require determining the regulatory valence of hemilineage TFs and defining whether they act on the same or different enhancers as birth order TFs.

sec.title: Fly husbandry:
sec.p: Flies were maintained on Bloomington food with a yeast sprinkle (“B” recipe, Lab Express, Ann Arbor, MI) at 25C on a 12:12 light-dark cycle with at least 60% humidity (provided by a beaker of water in the incubator). For staging developmental time points, pupae were marked at the 0hr APF “white pupae” stage. Animals for scRNAseq were collected +/−1–2 hours from the stated developmental time points (e.g. 48hr APF were collected from 47–49hr APF).
